questions.question: For BLS purposes, a 'child' is defined as:
options: B. 1 year to the onset of puberty
questions.rationale: A child is from 1 year of age to the onset of puberty. Under 1 year is an infant, and from puberty onward adult BLS is used.

questions.question: What is the correct compression depth for a child?
options: A. At least one-third the depth of the chest (about 2 inches / 5 cm)
questions.rationale: Child compressions are at least one-third the anterior-posterior depth of the chest, approximately 2 inches (5 cm).

questions.question: Two rescuers are performing CPR on a child. What compression-to-ventilation ratio should they use?
questions.rationale: With two or more rescuers, child CPR uses 15:2, providing more frequent ventilation than the adult 30:2.

questions.question: A single rescuer is performing CPR on a child. What ratio is used?
questions.rationale: A single rescuer uses 30:2 for a child; the ratio only changes to 15:2 when a second rescuer is available.

questions.question: A 5-year-old has a heart rate of 50/min with mottled skin and altered mental status despite effective oxygenation and ventilation. What should you do?
options: C. Wait and reassess in 5 minutes
D. Apply an AED and shock
questions.rationale: A heart rate below 60/min with poor perfusion despite adequate oxygenation/ventilation is an indication to start compressions in a child, because it rapidly leads to full arrest.

questions.question: Why is ventilation emphasized in pediatric CPR?
options: B. Most pediatric arrests are caused by respiratory failure or shock, so children are usually hypoxic
C. Children never have cardiac causes
D. Ventilation replaces compressions in children
questions.rationale: Pediatric arrests are typically the end result of respiratory failure or shock, so restoring oxygenation with breaths is especially important.

questions.question: How many hands may be used for chest compressions on a child?
options: C. One or two hands, whichever achieves adequate depth
questions.rationale: One or two hands may be used for a child, depending on the child's size, as long as adequate depth (one-third the chest) is achieved.

questions.question: A lone rescuer witnesses a previously healthy child suddenly collapse. What is the best sequence?
options: A. Give 2 minutes of CPR, then call for help
B. Activate the emergency response and get an AED first, then start CPR
D. Do nothing until help arrives
questions.rationale: A witnessed sudden collapse may be a cardiac (shockable) event, so a lone rescuer should activate the emergency response and get an AED first, then begin CPR.

questions.question: A lone rescuer finds a child unresponsive from an unwitnessed collapse. What is the recommended sequence?
options: A. Call for help and get an AED before any CPR
B. Provide about 2 minutes of CPR first, then activate the emergency response and get an AED
D. Wait to see if the child recovers
questions.rationale: An unwitnessed pediatric collapse is likely hypoxic, so a lone rescuer provides about 2 minutes of CPR first, then activates the emergency response and retrieves an AED.

questions.question: Where can you check for a pulse in a child?
options: D. There is no pulse check in children
questions.rationale: In a child, the carotid (neck) or femoral (groin) artery is used for the pulse check, limited to 10 seconds.

questions.question: Which statement about compression-only CPR in children is correct?
options: A. It is always preferred over CPR with breaths
B. Conventional CPR with breaths is preferred; compression-only is a fallback
C. Children should never receive compressions
D. Breaths are contraindicated in children
questions.rationale: Because pediatric arrest is usually hypoxic, CPR with breaths is preferred. Compression-only CPR is used only when a rescuer cannot or will not provide ventilation.

questions.question: What compression rate is used for a child?
questions.rationale: The compression rate for a child is 100–120/min, the same as for adults and infants.

questions.question: Only adult AED pads are available for a 6-year-old in a shockable arrest. What is correct?
options: B. Use the adult pads, ensuring they do not touch (front/back if needed)
C. Wait for pediatric pads regardless of delay
questions.rationale: Pediatric pads or a pediatric setting are preferred, but if unavailable, adult pads are used rather than withholding defibrillation, placed so they do not touch.

questions.question: A rescuer is compressing a child too shallowly out of fear of causing harm. What is the consequence?
options: A. Better outcomes due to gentleness
B. Inadequate blood flow because depth is insufficient
questions.rationale: Compressions that are too shallow do not generate adequate circulation. Depth must reach at least one-third the chest to be effective, even though it may feel forceful.

questions.question: Which best explains why the 2-rescuer child ratio differs from the adult ratio?
options: B. Pediatric arrests are usually hypoxic, so children benefit from more frequent ventilation (15:2)
D. There is no physiological reason
questions.rationale: Because hypoxia is the usual cause of pediatric arrest, more frequent ventilation (15:2 with two rescuers) benefits children, whereas adults retain 30:2.

questions.question: A child is unresponsive with a pulse of 100/min but is not breathing. What is the correct action?
options: B. Provide breaths at 1 every 2–3 seconds and reassess
C. Apply an AED and shock
D. Place in the recovery position
questions.rationale: With an adequate pulse but no breathing, provide rescue breathing at the pediatric rate of 1 breath every 2–3 seconds and reassess the pulse; compressions are not indicated with a good pulse.

questions.question: What defines the upper age boundary of 'child' in BLS?
questions.rationale: The child category extends to the onset of puberty (e.g., chest/underarm hair in males, breast development in females), after which adult BLS is used.

questions.question: During 2-rescuer child CPR, which is the correct combination?
options: A. Rate 100–120/min, depth one-third chest, ratio 15:2
D. Rate 80/min, depth one-quarter chest, ratio 15:1
questions.rationale: Correct 2-rescuer child CPR is 100–120/min, depth at least one-third the chest (~2 in/5 cm), full recoil, with a 15:2 ratio.
